hints.drachmas: 'Drachmas' is a noun meaning 'drachma coins' in the accusative

hints.perfect passive participle: A perfect passive participle would end in -tus/-ta/-tum — 'habens' ends in -ens
hints.future active participle: A future active participle would end in -urus/-ura — 'habens' ends in -ens
hints.gerundive: A gerundive would end in -endus/-enda — 'habens' is a present participle

hints.habito, habitare, habitavi, habitatum: These are the parts of 'habitare' (to dwell)
hints.abeo, abire, abii, abitum: These are the parts of 'abire' (to go away)
hints.teneo, tenere, tenui, tentum: These are the parts of 'tenere' (to hold) — a synonym

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'mulierem' — 'mulier' is the nominative form
hints.vocative: Vocative would also be 'mulier' but here she is the subject, not being addressed

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'mulieris' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'mulieris' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'mulieris' ends in -is

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'drachmae' — the -as ending is accusative plural (1st decl.)
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'drachmis' — 'drachmas' with -as is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'drachmarum' — 'drachmas' is accusative

hints.1st/2nd declension adjective: 'Decem' does not decline — it is an indeclinable cardinal number
hints.adverb: 'Decem' modifies the noun 'drachmas' as a number, not as an adverb

translation: What woman having ten drachma coins
